column3: Mechanism_or_notes
column4: Citation

column3: Sulfate analog that competitively inhibits the enzyme sulfate adenylyl transferase (Sat); molybdate and other oxyanions (tungstate, selenate, arsenate, chromate) act as analogs of sulfate and inhibit Sat, halting energy conservation in sulfate reducers. Perchlorate shows highest affinity while nitrate is lowest.
column4: ISME Communications 2021 notes that sulfate analogs such as molybdate, tungstate, selenate, arsenate and chromate and oxyanions like nitrate, chlorate and perchlorate are the best‑studied inhibitors of dissimilatory sulfate‑reducing microorganisms; these compounds inhibit sulfate adenylyl transferase, and perchlorate has the highest affinity while nitrate has the lowesthttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9723548/#:~:text=Selective%20inhibitors%20of%20dissimilatory%20sulfate,5%20%2C%20%2032.

column3: Sulfate analog that competes with sulfate for the Sat enzyme, inhibiting energy metabolism in sulfate‑reducing bacteria.
column4: ISME Communications 2021 identifies tungstate with other oxyanions (molybdate, selenate, chromate, arsenate) as selective inhibitors of sulfate reducers through inhibition of sulfate adenylyl transferasehttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9723548/#:~:text=Selective%20inhibitors%20of%20dissimilatory%20sulfate,5%20%2C%20%2032.

column3: Acts as a sulfate analog that binds to sulfate adenylyl transferase, blocking sulfate activation and inhibiting growth.
column4: The same 2021 study lists selenate among sulfate analogs that inhibit sulfate adenylyl transferase in sulfate‑reducing microorganismshttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9723548/#:~:text=Selective%20inhibitors%20of%20dissimilatory%20sulfate

column3: Sulfate analog; inhibits sulfate adenylyl transferase and thus energy metabolism of sulfate‑reducing bacteria.
column4: The sulfate adenylyl transferase study states that arsenate and other sulfate analogs inhibit sulfate reducers by acting on Sathttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9723548/#:~:text=Selective%20inhibitors%20of%20dissimilatory%20sulfate

column3: Chromate is a sulfate analog; competitive inhibitor of sulfate adenylyl transferase, reducing sulfate reduction.
column4: Chromate is included among oxyanion inhibitors of sulfate reducers that act by inhibiting sulfate adenylyl transferasehttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9723548/#:~:text=Selective%20inhibitors%20of%20dissimilatory%20sulfate

column3: An oxyanion that competes with sulfate for the Sat enzyme; has a relatively low affinity (higher IC50) but still inhibits sulfate reduction.
column4: The ISME Communications study notes that nitrate, chlorate and perchlorate are inhibitors of dissimilatory sulfate‑reducing microorganisms by targeting sulfate adenylyl transferase; nitrate has the lowest affinity among these inhibitorshttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9723548/#:~:text=Selective%20inhibitors%20of%20dissimilatory%20sulfate,5%20%2C%20%2032.

column3: For sulfate reducers, chlorate is an oxyanion inhibitor of sulfate adenylyl transferase. For nitrification, chlorate specifically inhibits the second step (oxidation of nitrite to nitrate) by acting as a substrate analogue for nitrite oxidoreductase, causing accumulation of nitrite and inhibiting comammox Nitrospira growth.
column4: The sulfate adenylyl transferase paper lists chlorate as one of the oxyanions that inhibit sulfate‑reducing microorganismshttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9723548/#:~:text=Selective%20inhibitors%20of%20dissimilatory%20sulfate,5%20%2C%20%2032. A 2024 Frontiers in Microbiology article explains that chlorate specifically inhibits the second step of nitrification, the oxidation of NO₂⁻ to NO₃⁻, and is used in vitro to reveal contributions of different nitrifiershttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11130707/#:~:text=Di%20et%20al,The%20effect%20of.

column3: Perchlorate competes with sulfate for sulfate adenylyl transferase; among oxyanion inhibitors it has the highest affinity (IC₅₀ ~0.14 mM) and strongly inhibits sulfate‑reducing bacteria.
column4: According to the ISME Communications study, perchlorate is the most potent sulfate analog inhibitor of sulfate adenylyl transferase, with a low IC₅₀, while nitrate is the weakesthttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9723548/#:~:text=Selective%20inhibitors%20of%20dissimilatory%20sulfate,5%20%2C%20%2032.

column3: Copper‑chelating nitrification inhibitor that binds the ammonia monooxygenase (AMO) enzyme; at ~86 µM it completely inhibited ammonia oxidation and N₂O emission in activated sludge, and it decreases AOB abundance and activity in soils.
column4: A study on selective inhibition of nitrification reports that 86 µM allylthiourea completely inhibited ammonia oxidation and N₂O emissionhttps://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23648864/#:~:text=Nitrification%20in%20wastewater%20treatment%20emits,The%20inhibition. The AMO inhibition mechanism is further supported by evidence that allylthiourea, a copper chelator, inhibits the bacterial ammonia monooxygenasehttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7170481/#:~:text=different%20nuclearities%20located%20on%20separate,50%E2%80%93%20%2052.

column3: Electron transport inhibitor; at 24 µM it selectively inhibits nitrite oxidation without affecting ammonia oxidation, leading to accumulation of nitrite.
column4: In activated sludge and enrichment cultures, 24 µM sodium azide selectively inhibited nitrite oxidation while ammonia oxidation and N₂O emission continuedhttps://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23648864/#:~:text=Nitrification%20in%20wastewater%20treatment%20emits,The%20inhibition.

column2: Ammonia oxidation (nitrification) by AOB and comammox Nitrospira
column3: Nitrification inhibitor that deactivates the ammonia monooxygenase enzyme; reduces growth of ammonia‑oxidizing bacteria and comammox Nitrospira.
column4: A Frontiers in Microbiology review notes that the nitrification inhibitor dicyandiamide (DCD) inhibits the growth of ammonia‑oxidizing bacteria by deactivating the ammonia monooxygenase enzyme, and DCD decreased the growth of comammox Nitrospira in microcosm studieshttps://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2022.1048735/full#:~:text=Little%20is%20known%20about%20the,addition%20of%20nitrogen%20fertilizers%20but.

column3: Heterocyclic nitrification inhibitor that targets ammonia‑oxidizing bacteria (AOB); decreases AOB abundance and metabolic activity, slowing conversion of NH₄⁺ to NO₃⁻. DMPP adsorbs to soil particles and is more effective in alkaline soils.
column4: A 2016 Applied and Environmental Microbiology study showed that DMPP inhibited ammonia oxidation and nitrous oxide production in alkaline vegetable soil by reducing AOB abundance and metabolic activity; the inhibitor had little effect in acidic pasture soil due to adsorption to soil particleshttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4988206/.

column3: Widely used nitrification inhibitor; inhibits the microbial enzyme ammonia monooxygenase (AMO), the first step in nitrification. Works by binding to the AMO enzyme and blocking oxidation of NH₄⁺ to NH₂OH.
column4: A U.S. Geological Survey review notes that in soils and water, nitrapyrin inhibits the activity of ammonia monooxygenase, the microbial enzyme that catalyzes the first step of nitrification from ammonium to nitritehttps://pubs.usgs.gov/publication/70217226#:~:text=Agricultural%20production%20and%20associated%20applications,range%20from%20reduced%20nitrate%20leaching.

column2: Ammonia oxidation (bacterial and archaeal AMO); Anammox; Denitrification (N₂O reductase)
column3: Alkyne gas that irreversibly inactivates ammonia monooxygenase by forming a covalent adduct; used at low partial pressures to inhibit nitrification. It also inhibits anaerobic ammonium oxidation (anammox) and nitrous oxide reductase in denitrification. In anammox assays, ~30 µM acetylene caused 95% inhibition of ammonium oxidation. Acetylene blocks nitrous oxide reductase, preventing N₂O reduction to N₂.
column4: Research on ammonia monooxygenase shows that acetylene and 1‑alkynes irreversibly inhibit AMO by forming a covalent adducthttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7170481/#:~:text=different%20nuclearities%20located%20on%20separate,50%E2%80%93%20%2052. In marine sediments, acetylene inhibited ammonium oxidation through the anammox pathway with a rate decay constant of 0.10 µM⁻¹ and 95% inhibition at ~30 µMhttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1907100/#:~:text=The%20effects%20of%20three%20metabolic,thus%203%20to%204%20mM. Acetylene also blocks the action of nitrous oxide reductase, so N₂O becomes the terminal product of denitrificationhttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2725510/#:~:text=20%20%2C%20%2042,be%20quantified%20by%20gas%20chromatography.

column3: Terminal alkynes that act as suicide inhibitors of ammonia monooxygenase; they form a covalent adduct at the active site and different chain lengths show differential inhibition between bacterial and archaeal AMOs.
column4: A review of ammonia monooxygenase inhibition reports that 1‑alkynes (C₂–C₈) irreversibly inhibit the enzyme by forming a covalent adduct; archaeal AMO showed differential sensitivity to chain length and phenylacetylene compared with bacterial AMOhttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7170481/#:~:text=different%20nuclearities%20located%20on%20separate

column3: Terminal alkynes that inactivate ammonia monooxygenase; phenylacetylene exhibited differential inhibition, affecting archaeal AMO more strongly than bacterial AMO.
column4: The same study reports that phenylacetylene and other 1‑alkynes inhibit ammonia monooxygenase and that archaeal AMO is more sensitive to certain alkynes than bacterial AMOhttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7170481/#:~:text=different%20nuclearities%20located%20on%20separate

column3: Structural analog of coenzyme M; acts as a suicide substrate for the coenzyme M methyl transferase in methanogens. At 1 µmol/ml BES completely inhibits methanogenesis from acetate and at 50 µmol/ml it inhibits hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis.
column4: An early AEM study showed that 1 µmol/mL BES completely inhibited methanogenesis from acetate in a thermophilic anaerobic digester and that 50 µmol/mL BES was needed to inhibit CO₂ reduction to methanehttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC240241/#:~:text=The%20effects%20of%202

column3: Halomethane that reacts with cobalamin (vitamin B₁₂) and other corrinoid coenzymes in methanogens, blocking methyl transfer reactions. Used in ruminant and gut microbiome studies to suppress methane production.
column4: A 2016 Applied and Environmental Microbiology article reports that bromochloromethane is an inhibitor of methanogenesis; it acts by reacting with cobalamin and reduces methanogen abundance in the rat guthttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4725281/.

column2: Methane oxidation (methanotrophy) and methanogenesis (at high concentrations)
column3: Halomethane used to selectively inhibit aerobic methane oxidation. At ~1 kPa it inhibits methane oxidation and also inhibits aceticlastic methanogens; at lower concentrations it suppresses methanotrophy more than methanogenesis.
column4: A 1997 AEM study found that about 1 kPa methyl fluoride inhibited aerobic methane oxidation and methane production in aceticlastic methanogens, but at lower concentrations it selectively inhibited methanotrophy while methanogenesis was less affectedhttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1389292/.

column3: Halomethane that reversibly inhibits methane monooxygenase at low partial pressures (~0.03 kPa). It blocks methane oxidation by methanotrophs without affecting methanogenesis.
column4: A 1998 AEM article showed that difluoromethane at low partial pressures (0.03 kPa) reversibly inhibited methane oxidation in Methylococcus capsulatus and in soils without affecting methanogenesis, making it an improved selective inhibitor of methanotrophyhttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC106652/#:~:text=Difluoromethane%20%28HFC,low%20concentrations%2C%20its%20lack%20of.

column3: Low‑molecular‑weight alcohol that specifically inhibits anammox bacteria. A decay constant of ~0.79 mM⁻¹ means 3–4 mM methanol is required for nearly complete inhibition; this concentration can stimulate denitrification.
column4: In an experiment on marine sediment, methanol specifically inhibited anammox with a decay constant of 0.79 mM⁻¹; 3–4 mM methanol was required for nearly complete inhibition and this level stimulated denitrificationhttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1907100/#:~:text=constant%2C%200,the%20process%20in%20wastewater%20bioreactors.

column2: Na⁺‑translocating NADH‑quinone oxidoreductase (Na‑NQR) in Gram‑negative bacteria (e.g., Vibrio cholerae)
column3: Natural antibiotic from pseudoalteromonads that specifically binds the Na‑pumping NADH:quinone oxidoreductase (Na‑NQR). Binding disrupts electron transport and promotes formation of reactive oxygen species, leading to death of bacteria possessing Na‑NQR. Korormicin is potent against Vibrio cholerae and other pathogens that rely on Na‑NQR but has little effect on organisms lacking the enzyme.
column4: A 2019 Journal of Bacteriology study reports that Korormicin is a potent and specific inhibitor of the Na⁺‑translocating NADH:quinone oxidoreductase (Na‑NQR) complex. Binding to Na‑NQR promotes formation of reactive oxygen species, and the inhibitor is effective against pathogens that rely on Na‑NQR, including Vibrio choleraehttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6509656/#:~:text=only%20in%20prokaryotes%2C%20the%20Na%2B,2S%20center%20cofactors%20with%20O_%7B2.

column3: Also acts as an inhibitor of methane monooxygenase; used to measure methane oxidation rates. At certain partial pressures it inhibits methane oxidation more than methanogenesis.
column4: The 1997 AEM study notes that acetylene (like other alkynes) irreversibly inhibits ammonia monooxygenase; acetylene is also used to inhibit methane oxidation in methanotrophic assays and can affect methanogenesis at higher concentrationshttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1389292/https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7170481/#:~:text=different%20nuclearities%20located%20on%20separate
